metadata.stance: Game helper reference for VTOL VR. Values marked heuristic are Betty estimates for routing, comparison, and solver use, not official flight manual data.

aircraft.name: F/A-26B
notes: Large hardpoint count makes it the default recommendation for heavy A2A or multirole loadouts.
Conformal fuel tanks add fuel endurance but add mass and should be traded against maneuver performance.

aircraft.name: F-45A
notes: External hardpoints increase load but reduce stealth.

aircraft.name: AV-42C
notes: Useful for VTOL transport/attack tasks, fewer weapon slots than the F/A-26B.

aircraft.name: T-55 Tyro
notes: Two-seat trainer that can carry many weapon and sensor types.

aircraft.name: AH-94
notes: Attack helicopter focused on hovering, sensors, and close target engagement.

aircraft.name: EF-24G Mischief
notes: Specialized EW platform with sensors and ECM pods for finding, targeting, and disrupting emissions.

loadout_recommendations.name: Balanced F/A-26B A2A CAP

stores.purpose: endurance if the sortie is long

tradeoffs: Good default for PvE CAP and intercepts.
Drop tanks/CFT increase endurance but reduce acceleration and turn performance.
If expecting merge-heavy fighting, trade two radar missiles for two extra IR missiles.

loadout_recommendations.name: Light F/A-26B fighter sweep
tradeoffs: Better acceleration and agility than a heavy missile truck.

loadout_recommendations.name: F/A-26B self-escort SEAD

stores.purpose: target ID and laser/sensor work if needed

tradeoffs: Good when you may need to fight through air threats before attacking SAM radars.
Less pure A2A endurance than a CAP loadout.

performance_graphs.title: F/A-26B stores drag / agility heuristic

x_axis.name: drag_index
x_axis.meaning: 0 clean, 1 light A2A, 2 balanced A2A, 3 heavy multirole

performance_graphs.solver_note: Linear interpolation is acceptable between points. Use for relative loadout comparison only.

performance_graphs.title: A2A missile employment quality by range band
performance_graphs.solver_note: This is a doctrine helper, not a probability-of-kill calculator. Favor radar missiles at medium/BVR, IR missiles near merge.

tactics.summary: Against radar missiles, combine geometry, terrain/clutter, and chaff. Notching places the threat near your 3 or 9 o'clock to reduce closure; chaff is most useful while notching.
Use the aircraft launch-zone cues when available. Betty should remind the pilot to check DLZ/no-escape cues rather than inventing exact ranges from incomplete telemetry.

lessons.title: Radar missile evasion brief
lessons.summary: Defeat radar missiles with geometry first, then countermeasures. Chaff helps most when you are notching or using terrain/clutter.
steps: Put the threat near your 3 or 9 o'clock to reduce closure and enter the notch.
Use chaff in short bursts while holding the notch; do not rely on chaff while flying straight at or away from the missile.
Drag or descend if you need time and space, but preserve enough speed to maneuver.
If terrain is available, mask behind it instead of trying to out-turn a missile in the open.
After the warning drops, recommit only when you have energy and situational awareness.

lessons.title: BVR air-to-air brief
lessons.summary: Use radar missiles to create pressure before the merge, but let the in-game launch-zone cues drive exact timing.
steps: Build altitude and speed before committing so the missile leaves with energy.
Sort targets on radar, confirm hostile or mission context, and avoid wasting shots on poor aspect targets.
Fire AIM-120 class weapons when the DLZ supports the shot; no-escape cues are better than fixed range rules.
Support the missile long enough to matter, then crank or defend instead of flying straight into return fire.
Keep IR missiles for the merge or defensive snapshots.

lessons.title: F/A-26B turning brief
lessons.summary: The F/A-26B likes an energy-managed rate fight more than a slow high-AoA wallow, especially when carrying missiles and fuel.
steps: Enter the turn fast enough to sustain energy; Betty's solver gives a current target speed from aircraft, fuel, and stores.
For sustained rate, keep flaps up or auto and avoid carrying landing-flap drag through the fight.
If very slow and you need nose authority for a snapshot, use extra flap briefly, then clean up as speed returns.
Watch AoA and buffet; if the jet is mushy, unload slightly and let it accelerate.
Jettison draggy tanks or heavy stores only when the mission and rules allow it.

lessons.title: Case I carrier recovery brief
lessons.summary: Fly the pattern deliberately: configure, stabilize, scan lineup and glideslope, and correct early rather than chasing late.
steps: Arrive with enough spacing and configure before the groove: gear down, landing flaps, hook as required.
Fly a stable downwind and final turn; do not arrive in the groove fast and dirty.
On final, scan lineup, angle, and descent rate; small corrections beat large last-second pulls.
Use power for glideslope and attitude for AoA-like speed control in the usual carrier pattern rhythm.
If unstable, go around instead of forcing the trap.
